```{r poly-conf}
  collect_predictions() %>%
  conf_mat(truth = diagnosis, estimate = .pred_class) %>%
  autoplot(type = "heatmap") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "darkgrey", high = "darkblue") +
  labs(title = "Polynomial Logistic Regression — Confusion Matrix (CV)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

ROC Curve
```{r poly-roc}
poly_cv %>%
  collect_predictions() %>%
  roc_curve(truth = diagnosis, .pred_Benign) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = 1 - specificity, y = sensitivity)) +
  geom_path(color = "darkblue", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_abline(lty = 2, color = "darkgrey") +
  labs(title = "Polynomial Logistic Regression — ROC Curve (CV)",
       x = "1 - Specificity", y = "Sensitivity") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

Full polynomial Key Takeaway

Under 10-fold cross-validation, the polynomial model achieves 92.92% accuracy, 95.06% ROC AUC, 93.63% sensitivity, and 91.73% specificity. While these are reasonable numbers in absolute terms, the model shows signs of estimation instability — the expanded feature space produces coefficients that fail to converge reliably, as evidenced by the NA coefficient table. This indicates that the polynomial terms introduced more noise than signal, and the added curvature did not capture meaningful nonlinear patterns beyond what the linear terms already explain. The relatively balanced sensitivity and specificity suggest the model is not strongly biased toward either class, but the overall discriminative power (ROC AUC = 95.06%) is noticeably lower than models operating on the original linear feature space. The high standard error on accuracy (0.0118) also reflects greater fold-to-fold variability, consistent with an overparameterized model that fits differently depending on which observations land in each fold.

## Reduced Polynomial Model

<div class="objectives-ribbon"> Objectives Met</div>

<div class="objective-item">
<strong>Objective 1:</strong> Describe probability as a foundation of statistical modeling, including inference and maximum likelihood estimation
</div>

<div class="objective-item">
<strong>Objective 3:</strong> Demonstrate model selection given a set of candidate models
</div>

<div class="objective-item">
<strong>Objective 5:</strong> Use programming software to fit and assess statistical models
</div>

<div class="objective-explanation">
The reduced polynomial model uses Lasso's top 4 predictors to build a smarter polynomial specification (8 terms instead of ~38), demonstrating <strong>model selection guided by prior results</strong>. MLE converges cleanly with no NA values, and the significant quadratic term for <code>texture_mean</code> (p = 0.04) reveals a genuine <strong>nonlinear relationship</strong> that linear models missed — while the other three predictors are adequately captured by linear terms alone.
</div>

The full polynomial model expanded all 19 predictors to ~38 features, overwhelming the training data and producing unstable estimates with weaker performance (92.92% accuracy, 95.06% ROC AUC). Rather than abandon polynomial terms entirely, we used our Lasso results to guide a smarter approach — restricting the expansion to only the four top Lasso-selected predictors (area_mean, concave_points_worst, area_se, texture_mean). This reduces the feature space to just 8 terms, combining Lasso's variable selection with polynomial expansion to test whether curvature improves the model without the noise.

Reduced Polynomial Recipe
```{r poly-reduced-recipe}
poly_reduced_recipe <- recipe(diagnosis ~ area_mean + concave_points_worst + 
                                area_se + texture_mean, 
                              data = cancer_train) %>%
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) %>%
  step_poly(all_numeric_predictors(), degree = 2)
```

Model Specification
```{r poly-reduced-spec}
poly_reduced_spec <- logistic_reg() %>%
  set_engine("glm") %>%
  set_mode("classification")

poly_reduced_wf <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(poly_reduced_recipe) %>%
  add_model(poly_reduced_spec)
```

Cross Validation
```{r poly-reduced-cv}
poly_reduced_cv <- poly_reduced_wf %>%
  fit_resamples(
    resamples = cancer_folds,
    metrics   = metric_set(accuracy, roc_auc, sensitivity, specificity),
    control   = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE)
  )

poly_reduced_cv %>%
  collect_metrics() %>%
  kable(caption = "Polynomial (Reduced: 4 Predictors) — 10-Fold CV Metrics", digits = 4) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```
Coefficients
```{r poly-reduced-fit}
poly_reduced_fit <- poly_reduced_wf %>%
  fit(data = cancer_train)

poly_reduced_fit %>%
  extract_fit_engine() %>%
  tidy() %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(., 4))) %>%
  kable(caption = "Polynomial (Reduced) Coefficients") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```

Confusion Matrix
```{r poly-reduced-conf}
poly_reduced_cv %>%
  collect_predictions() %>%
  conf_mat(truth = diagnosis, estimate = .pred_class) %>%
  autoplot(type = "heatmap") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "darkgrey", high = "darkblue") +
  labs(title = "Polynomial (Reduced) — Confusion Matrix (CV)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

Roc Curve
```{r poly-reduced-roc}
poly_reduced_cv %>%
  collect_predictions() %>%
  roc_curve(truth = diagnosis, .pred_Benign) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = 1 - specificity, y = sensitivity)) +
  geom_path(color = "darkblue", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_abline(lty = 2, color = "darkgrey") +
  labs(title = "Polynomial (Reduced) — ROC Curve (CV)",
       x = "1 - Specificity", y = "Sensitivity") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

Reduced Polynomial Key Takeaway

The reduced polynomial model, built on the four Lasso-selected predictors (area_mean, concave_points_worst, area_se, texture_mean), achieves 96.24% accuracy, 99.30% ROC AUC, 97.87% sensitivity, and 93.49% specificity. With only 8 features (4 predictors × 2 polynomial terms), the coefficient estimates converge cleanly — no NA values. Two terms reach statistical significance: concave_points_worst (linear, p < 0.0001) and texture_mean (both linear p = 0.0002 and quadratic p = 0.04), suggesting that texture_mean does exhibit a slight curved relationship with diagnosis. The quadratic terms for area_mean and area_se are not significant, indicating their relationship with log-odds is adequately captured by a straight line.


## Full and Reduced Polynomial Comparison 

```{r}
tibble::tribble(
  ~Metric,                  ~`Full (19 predictors, ~38 terms)`, ~`Reduced (4 predictors, 8 terms)`,
  "Accuracy",               "92.92%",                           "96.24%",
  "ROC AUC",                "95.06%",                           "99.30%",
  "Sensitivity",            "93.63%",                           "97.87%",
  "Specificity",            "91.73%",                           "93.49%",
  "Std Error (Accuracy)",   "0.0118",                           "0.0067",
  "Coefficients",           "NA (failed to converge)",          "All estimated cleanly"
) %>%
  kable(caption = "Full vs. Reduced Polynomial — Side-by-Side Comparison") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE) %>%
  column_spec(3, bold = TRUE)

```

The reduced polynomial model improves on every single metric — and does it with fewer than one-quarter of the features. The full model drowned in ~38 noisy terms, producing unstable estimates and weak discrimination. By letting Lasso identify the four most important predictors first, the reduced model concentrates polynomial expansion where it matters, achieves stable convergence, and recovers nearly all the discriminative power of the best linear models. The one meaningful finding is that texture_mean benefits from a quadratic term (p = 0.04), while the other three predictors are well-served by linear terms alone.

---

# Interaction Logistic Regression {.tabset}

<div class="objectives-ribbon"> Objectives Met</div>

<div class="objective-item">
<strong>Objective 1:</strong> Describe probability as a foundation of statistical modeling, including inference and maximum likelihood estimation
</div>

<div class="objective-item">
<strong>Objective 3:</strong> Demonstrate model selection given a set of candidate models
</div>

<div class="objective-item">
<strong>Objective 4:</strong> Express the results of statistical models to a general audience
</div>

<div class="objective-item">
<strong>Objective 5:</strong> Use programming software to fit and assess statistical models
</div>

<div class="objective-explanation">
Interaction terms test whether the effect of one predictor <strong>depends on the level of another</strong> — a hypothesis grounded in the probabilistic structure of the GLM. The non-significant interaction terms (p = 0.35 and p = 0.60) provide evidence for an <strong>additive model</strong>, a finding that is straightforward to <strong>communicate to a general audience</strong>: each predictor independently contributes to diagnosis without synergistic effects. This negative result is itself a form of model selection — confirming that the simpler additive model is preferred.
</div>

Interaction terms allow the effect of one predictor on diagnosis to depend on the level of another predictor. An example, the relationship between area_mean and malignancy might differ for tumors with high vs. low texture_mean. This captures synergistic effects that main-effects-only models miss.
I analysed the intercation effect between the 4 reduced variables to see whether they have some dependability.

Interaction Recipe
```{r}
interaction_reduced_recipe <- recipe(diagnosis ~ area_mean + concave_points_worst + 
                                      area_se + texture_mean, 
                                    data = cancer_train) %>%
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) %>%
  step_interact(terms = ~ area_mean:texture_mean + area_mean:concave_points_worst)

```
Model Specification and Workflow

```{r inter-spec}
inter_reduced_spec <- logistic_reg() %>%
  set_engine("glm") %>%
  set_mode("classification")

inter_reduced_wf <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(interaction_reduced_recipe) %>%
  add_model(inter_reduced_spec)
```

Cross-Validation
```{r inter-cv}
inter_reduced_cv <- inter_reduced_wf %>%
  fit_resamples(
    resamples = cancer_folds,
    metrics   = metric_set(accuracy, roc_auc, sensitivity, specificity),
    control   = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE)
  )

inter_reduced_cv %>%
  collect_metrics() %>%
  kable(caption = "Interaction (Reduced: 4 Predictors) — 10-Fold CV Metrics", digits = 4) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```

Fit the Final Model
```{r inter-fit}
inter_reduced_fit <- inter_reduced_wf %>%
  fit(data = cancer_train)

inter_reduced_fit %>%
  extract_fit_engine() %>%
  tidy() %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(., 4))) %>%
  arrange(p.value) %>%
  kable(caption = "Interaction (Reduced) — All Coefficients") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```
```{r}
inter_reduced_cv %>%
  collect_predictions() %>%
  conf_mat(truth = diagnosis, estimate = .pred_class) %>%
  autoplot(type = "heatmap") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "darkgrey", high = "darkblue") +
  labs(title = "Interaction (Reduced) — Confusion Matrix (CV)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```


ROC Curve
```{r inter-roc}
inter_reduced_cv %>%
  collect_predictions() %>%
  roc_curve(truth = diagnosis, .pred_Benign) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = 1 - specificity, y = sensitivity)) +
  geom_path(color = "darkblue", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_abline(lty = 2, color = "darkgrey") +
  labs(title = "Interaction (Reduced) — ROC Curve (CV)",
       x = "1 - Specificity", y = "Sensitivity") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

The reduced interaction model (6 features) achieves strong performance (95.58% accuracy, 99.37% ROC AUC), but neither interaction term is statistically significant (p = 0.35 and p = 0.60). All four main effects individually are highly significant. This confirms that the predictors contribute additively — tumor size, texture, concavity, and area variability each independently influence diagnosis without synergistic effects. The interaction terms add no meaningful predictive value beyond what the main effects already capture.

---

# Model Selection{.tabset}

<div class="objectives-ribbon"> Objectives Met</div>

<div class="objective-item">
<strong>Objective 3:</strong> Demonstrate model selection given a set of candidate models
</div>

<div class="objective-item">
<strong>Objective 4:</strong> Express the results of statistical models to a general audience
</div>

<div class="objective-item">
<strong>Objective 5:</strong> Use programming software to fit and assess statistical models
</div>

<div class="objective-explanation">
The grand comparison evaluates all nine candidate models under a <strong>unified 10-fold cross-validation framework</strong>, selecting Lasso as the winner based on accuracy, ROC AUC, sensitivity, and specificity. The comparison table is built <strong>programmatically</strong> from each model's CV object — no manual entry. The final recommendation — Lasso with 10 predictors achieving 97% accuracy — is <strong>expressed in terms a clinician can act on</strong>, fulfilling the goal of communicating statistical results to a general audience.
</div>

```{r}
# Models from fit_resamples (column = mean) 
fit_resample_models <- bind_rows(
  logistic_cv      %>% collect_metrics() %>% select(.metric, mean) %>% mutate(Model = "Full Logistic Regression"),
  reduced_cv       %>% collect_metrics() %>% select(.metric, mean) %>% mutate(Model = "Reduced Logistic Regression"),
  lda_cv           %>% collect_metrics() %>% select(.metric, mean) %>% mutate(Model = "LDA"),
  qda_cv           %>% collect_metrics() %>% select(.metric, mean) %>% mutate(Model = "QDA"),
  poly_cv          %>% collect_metrics() %>% select(.metric, mean) %>% mutate(Model = "Polynomial (Full)"),
  poly_reduced_cv  %>% collect_metrics() %>% select(.metric, mean) %>% mutate(Model = "Polynomial (Reduced)"),
  inter_reduced_cv %>% collect_metrics() %>% select(.metric, mean) %>% mutate(Model = "Interaction (Reduced)")
)

# Ridge & Lasso from tune_grid (column = .estimate)
tuned_models <- bind_rows(
  ridge_cv_metrics %>% select(.metric, .estimate) %>% rename(mean = .estimate) %>% mutate(Model = "Ridge"),
  lasso_cv_metrics %>% select(.metric, .estimate) %>% rename(mean = .estimate) %>% mutate(Model = "Lasso")
)

# Combine and pivot 
grand_table <- bind_rows(fit_resample_models, tuned_models) %>%
  filter(.metric %in% c("accuracy", "roc_auc", "sensitivity", "specificity")) %>%
  select(Model, .metric, mean) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = .metric, values_from = mean) %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(., 4))) %>%
  arrange(desc(accuracy))

grand_table %>%
  kable(caption = "Model Comparison — 10-Fold CV Performance",
        col.names = c("Model", "Accuracy", "ROC AUC", "Sensitivity", "Specificity")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(which(grand_table$Model == "Lasso"), bold = TRUE, background = "#d4edda")
```

## Model Selection Summary

I evaluated nine models using 10-fold cross-validation. Lasso logistic regression was the top performer (97.13% accuracy, 99.50% ROC AUC) after retaining 10 of 19 predictors through its L1 penalty — dropping 9 redundant features while achieving the highest scores on every metric. Reduced models consistently outperformed their full counterparts, reinforcing that fewer, well-chosen features beat more features at every level. LDA captured the most malignant cases (98.94% sensitivity) but at the cost of more false positives, while QDA offered a more balanced tradeoff. Ridge mirrored standard logistic regression, confirming that multicollinearity was already handled during preprocessing. The full polynomial model was the weakest due to overparameterization, and interaction terms were not significant, confirming an additive relationship among predictors. Based on these results, I selected Lasso as the final model — 10 predictors, 97% accuracy, and the strongest balance of performance and simplicity.

---

Participation Reflection 

<div class="objective-explanation">
Throughout Statistical Modeling I, I participated actively and consistently in class and group discussions. I regularly asked clarifying questions, challenged ideas when appropriate, and contributed to conversations about modeling decisions and interpretation. 
<br><br>
My preparation outside of class reviewing readings, practicing code, and experimenting with workflows allowed me to contribute meaningfully during group discussions and collaborate effectively with peers. Overall, my participation reflected curiosity, engagement, and a commitment to building a supportive learning environment while strengthening my own understanding of statistical modeling.
</div>

<script>
window.onscroll = function() {
  let btn = document.getElementById("back-to-top");
  if (document.body.scrollTop > 300 || document.documentElement.scrollTop > 300) {
    btn.style.display = "block";
  } else {
    btn.style.display = "none";
  }
};

function scrollToTop() {
  window.scrollTo({top: 0, behavior: 'smooth'});
}
</script>

<div id="back-to-top" onclick="scrollToTop()">Back to Top</div>
